text: fat is 12g. saturated fat is one of the main pressure points. for dairy, the fat side can either add richness or drag the whole profile down.
carbs is 3g. sugar, glycemic index, and starch are doing most of the work. for a dairy food, that is a strong sign.
protein is 11.3g. protein quality is clearly one of the selling points. for dairy, useful protein can rescue a lot of weaker traits.
vitamin b12 at 13% DV, vitamin a at 10% DV stand out most. that is not enough to move the verdict very far on its own.
calcium at 21% DV, zinc at 8% DV, magnesium at 2% DV stand out most. that stays fairly limited overall.

the upsides first. easy protein delivery for normal diets because dairy gets real credit for convenience. clear calcium identity because that is a core category strength. slots into meals very easily because practical versatility matters a lot for dairy.

on the weaker side. not everyone tolerates dairy well because that is a real use-case limitation. fat quality varies wildly across dairy because some dairy is clean, some really is not. processing changes the category a lot because the label alone does not tell the whole story.
Ricotta is B tier. Best when the protein payoff or fermentation angle outweighs the fat and sodium tradeoffs.
